content: {"recommendations": [{"section_id": "s01", "section_title": "Overview and Definitions", "diagram_type": "comparison_matrix", "description": "Side-by-side comparison of the principal intermittent fasting protocols described in the section: time-restricted eating (16:8, 14:10), 5:2 modified fasting (500-600 kcal on two non-consecutive days), alternate-day fasting (fast day vs feed day), and periodic/prolonged fasting plus the fasting-mimicking diet, contrasting the duration, frequency, and severity of the fasting interval for each."}, {"section_id": "s03", "section_title": "Mechanisms of Action", "diagram_type": "causal_chain", "description": "Causal chain showing how glycogen depletion after 12-16 hours of fasting lowers glucose and insulin, drives adipose lipolysis and hepatic beta-oxidation to produce beta-hydroxybutyrate, which inhibits class I HDACs and converges with AMPK activation and mTORC1 suppression to modulate oxidative stress resistance, mitochondrial biogenesis, and inflammatory tone."}, {"section_id": "s05", "section_title": "Safety Profile and Risks", "diagram_type": "hierarchy", "description": "Hierarchical classification of adverse events discussed in the section: transient symptomatic effects (headache, fatigue, dizziness, irritability, constipation, hunger, sleep disturbance) versus uncommon serious concerns (symptomatic hypoglycemia, dehydration, electrolyte disturbances, gallstone formation, unmasking of disordered eating) versus behavioral effects (binge-like rebound eating), organized by severity and time course."}, {"section_id": "s06", "section_title": "Special Populations", "diagram_type": "comparison_matrix", "description": "Comparison matrix across the special populations examined in the section (adults with type 2 diabetes, women with PCOS, metabolic syndrome patients) showing expected benefits (HbA1c and fasting glucose reduction, insulin sensitivity, androgen and menstrual improvements) against population-specific hazards such as iatrogenic hypoglycemia on sulfonylureas or insulin and the need for medication de-escalation and clinician supervision."}, {"section_id": "s08", "section_title": "Clinical Implications", "diagram_type": "decision_tree", "description": "Decision tree for clinicians operationalizing intermittent fasting: starting from a candidate adult with metabolic syndrome, insulin resistance, or obesity, branching on contraindications (pregnancy, lactation, eating disorder history), then on patient preference and schedule to select 16:8 time-restricted eating, 5:2 modified fasting, or alternate-day fasting, framed as one option rather than a first-line prescription."}]}
